title: The Shape of Discovery: Detecting Topological Precursors to Technological Breakthroughs in the USPTO Patent Citation Network
description: Applies persistent homology to the U.S. patent citation network (~8M patents, ~118M citations, 1976-2023) to test whether topological signatures precede technological breakthroughs. The central finding is null: an apparent precursor signal (Wilcoxon p < 0.001, N=57 valid comparisons from 65-entry catalog) is entirely explained by a temporal confound — beta-1 (H1 feature counts) declines universally at ~1.8/year across all 28 CPC section pairs due to network maturation. Z-scores correlate almost perfectly with filing year (Spearman rho = -0.921, p < 1e-20). After per-pair linear detrending, all significance vanishes (Wilcoxon p = 0.85). The project demonstrates three findings: (1) the precursor hypothesis does not survive temporal detrending; (2) the patent knowledge landscape is systematically flattening over time (genuine structural change, confirmed after density control); (3) temporal trends in TDA on evolving networks produce spurious signals — a methodological cautionary tale. Seven confound robustness checks implemented. Conceptual design, analytical framework, and implementation by Claude (Opus 4.6, Anthropic) via claude.ai. Implementation by Claude Code. Facilitated by Christopher Ortiz.
notes: Conceived by Claude (Opus 4.6, Anthropic) via claude.ai. Implementation by Claude Code. This project represents a novel application of persistent homology to patent citation networks. The central finding is null after controlling for a temporal confound: beta-1 declines universally across all CPC pairs, and the apparent precursor signal is a temporal artifact. The methodological lesson — that temporal trends in TDA on evolving networks produce spurious signals — is the primary contribution.
